questions.stem: Screening: how do you distinguish a process from a thread when the interviewer asks what shares what?
options: Threads in a process share address space; processes usually do not
Processes share one address space; threads each get a private heap
Threads and processes are identical isolation units
Only processes can run concurrently on multicore CPUs
questions.explanation: Threads share the process address space; separate processes get isolated spaces.

questions.stem: What does a PCB primarily hold that the kernel needs on every context switch?
options: Only the executable path string of the program
Register set, PID, scheduling state, memory maps, open files
Only open socket buffers for networking
Only the user-facing process name for ps
questions.explanation: The PCB is the kernel's bookkeeping for execution state and resources.

questions.stem: FCFS scheduling: what tradeoff do you call out in a screening interview?
options: Always optimal average wait with no starvation risk ever
Preempts long jobs every fixed quantum automatically
Simple and fair by arrival, but convoy effect can hurt short jobs
Requires priority numbers from the user at submit time
questions.explanation: FCFS is easy but a long first job delays everyone behind it.

questions.stem: SJF vs FCFS: when would you argue SJF wins on average waiting time in an interview?
options: Never; FCFS always has lower average wait
Only when all jobs have identical burst lengths
Only on single-core machines with no I/O
When short jobs can run before long ones, cutting average wait
questions.explanation: Preferring shorter bursts reduces mean wait; needs burst estimates.

questions.stem: Round-robin: what does the time quantum control that you should explain clearly?
options: Disk block size for the file system only
Responsiveness vs context-switch overhead balance
Maximum number of processes allowed in memory
Nothing; RR ignores quantum and runs to completion
questions.explanation: Small quantum feels interactive; too small wastes CPU on switches.

questions.stem: Priority scheduling: which starvation issue do you mention and how is it often fixed?
options: Low-priority jobs may wait forever; aging raises priority over time
High-priority jobs always starve; fix by disabling priorities
Starvation only happens with FCFS, never with priorities
Aging lowers priority of interactive jobs to favor batch
questions.explanation: Without aging, low-priority work can be postponed indefinitely.

questions.stem: Mutex vs semaphore: how do you explain the everyday distinction in one line?
options: They are identical; names differ only by language
Semaphores only protect disk I/O; mutexes only protect network
Mutex is binary ownership lock; semaphore counts permits (can be >1)
Mutexes allow multiple holders; semaphores allow exactly one
questions.explanation: Mutex is typically exclusive ownership; counting semaphores gate N slots.

questions.stem: What is a monitor in the classic sync sense when you contrast it with raw locks?
options: A hardware performance counter for CPU utilization
A kernel thread that only polls disk interrupts
A page-replacement algorithm related to LRU
A language/runtime construct bundling shared data with condition waits
questions.explanation: Monitors encapsulate mutual exclusion and condition variables together.

questions.stem: Name the four Coffman conditions for deadlock that interviewers expect.
options: Paging, segmentation, thrashing, and TLB miss
Mutual exclusion, hold-and-wait, no preemption, circular wait
FCFS, SJF, RR, and multilevel feedback
Polling, interrupt, DMA, and context switch
questions.explanation: All four must hold simultaneously for classic resource deadlock.

questions.stem: Banker's algorithm at a high level: what problem is it trying to avoid?
options: Disk fragmentation by compacting every allocation
Cache thrashing by pinning all pages in RAM
Unsafe resource grants that could lead to deadlock
Priority inversion by boosting every waiter
questions.explanation: It admits requests only if a safe sequence still exists afterward.

questions.stem: Paging vs segmentation: what contrast should you lead with in a systems interview?
options: Paging uses fixed-size frames; segmentation uses variable logical parts
Segmentation always uses fixed 4KB units like pages
Paging and segmentation are the same address translation
Only segmentation needs a page table walk
questions.explanation: Pages are uniform blocks; segments follow program structure sizes.

questions.stem: What does a TLB do, and why do interviewers care so much about its hit rate?
options: Caches disk sectors to avoid reading from SSD
Caches recent VPN→PFN translations to skip slow page-table walks
Stores the full process image so context switches are free
Schedules threads across cores using work stealing
questions.explanation: Misses force expensive page-table lookups; locality keeps hit rate high.

questions.stem: Thrashing: how do you describe the symptom and the root cause briefly?
options: CPU idle because no runnable processes exist
Network congestion from too many TCP connections
Excessive paging so little useful work runs between page faults
Deadlock from circular wait on mutexes only
questions.explanation: Working sets exceed RAM; the system spends time swapping, not computing.

questions.stem: Virtual memory: what promise do you articulate to a screening interviewer?
options: RAM is unused; everything always lives only on disk
Each process gets physical addresses with no translation
Virtual memory removes the need for a page table
Programs see a large contiguous address space backed by RAM+disk
questions.explanation: VM abstracts physical limits via mapping and demand paging.

questions.stem: Interrupts vs polling: when do you prefer interrupts in a systems discussion?
options: Always poll; interrupts are never used on modern CPUs
When devices are rare/idle and you want CPU free until an event
Only for keyboard input; disks must always poll
Polling is always lower latency than interrupts
questions.explanation: Interrupts avoid busy-waiting; polling can win for very high event rates.

questions.stem: User mode vs kernel mode: what security boundary are you protecting in practice?
options: Privileged instructions and hardware so apps cannot corrupt the system
Only the color theme of the desktop UI
Whether a process may open a text file for reading
The difference between TCP and UDP sockets only
questions.explanation: Kernel mode can touch privileged ops; user mode traps via syscalls.

questions.stem: Why is a context switch expensive enough that interviewers expect you to mention it?
options: It is free; modern CPUs never save registers
Only network packets are flushed; CPU state is untouched
Saving/restoring registers, switching address spaces/TLB, and cache effects
It only renames the process without touching hardware state
questions.explanation: State save/restore plus MMU and cache disruption cost real cycles.

questions.stem: Demand paging: what happens on a first reference to a non-resident page?
options: The CPU invents zeros without involving the OS
The process is killed immediately with no recovery
The TLB is disabled permanently for that process
A page fault loads the page (or allocates) then resumes the process
questions.explanation: Fault handler brings the page in (or COW/zero-fill) then restarts.

questions.stem: What is the working set, and why does it matter so much under memory pressure?
options: All files currently open across the entire machine
The set of pages a process actively uses over a recent window
The list of ready queues in the scheduler only
Every page ever touched since boot, never shrinking
questions.explanation: If working sets do not fit in RAM, thrashing becomes likely.

questions.stem: Syscall path: why must user code enter the kernel instead of calling drivers directly?
options: User code can freely execute privileged I/O instructions
Syscalls exist only for debugging and are optional on production
Isolation and mediation: the kernel validates and performs privileged work
Drivers live entirely in user space with no kernel gate
questions.explanation: Syscalls are the controlled trap into privileged, audited kernel paths.

questions.stem: You design a lock-free single-producer queue; interviewer asks when a mutex is still the better interview answer—what do you say?
options: When correctness under contention and simplicity beat micro-optimizing atomics
Mutexes are obsolete; always prefer lock-free atomics
Mutexes only work on single-core machines
Lock-free code never needs memory ordering concerns
questions.explanation: Mutexes are clearer and often fast enough; lock-free is harder to get right.

questions.stem: Priority inversion appears in a RT discussion: how do you explain the classic failure mode?
options: High-priority threads always run immediately regardless of locks
A low-priority holder of a lock blocks a high-priority waiter while medium work runs
Inversion only happens with RR scheduling, never with priorities
It means the TLB flushed the wrong address space
questions.explanation: Medium work preempts the low holder, delaying the high waiter; priority inheritance helps.

questions.stem: Multilevel feedback queue: what policy intuition should you convey to the interviewer?
options: One fixed priority forever based on arrival order only
All queues always use FCFS with infinite quantum
Jobs start interactive-friendly; CPU hogs demote to lower queues
Feedback queues ignore I/O-bound behavior completely
questions.explanation: MLFQ adapts: short/interactive stay responsive; long jobs get lower priority.

questions.stem: You hold lock A then try lock B; another thread holds B then tries A—what do you diagnose?
options: Normal lock elision with no risk
A livelock that always resolves within one quantum
Only a TLB shootdown race, not a lock issue
Classic deadlock via circular wait on two locks
questions.explanation: Opposite lock orders create circular wait; fix with a global lock order.

questions.stem: Condition variable misuse: why must you recheck the predicate after wait returns?
options: Wait never returns spuriously; the predicate is always true
Spurious wakes and races mean the condition may still be false
Predicates are checked only by the kernel scheduler
Rechecking is needed only for semaphores, never CVs
questions.explanation: Always wait in a loop on the invariant; wakes are not a proof of readiness.

questions.stem: Copy-on-write after fork: what do you say happens to pages until a write?
options: Parent and child share read-only mappings; write faults create private copies
Fork always deep-copies the entire address space immediately
Child gets an empty address space with no shared pages
COW only applies to kernel stacks, never user pages
questions.explanation: COW defers copying until mutation, making fork cheap for exec-heavy paths.

questions.stem: Page replacement: when is LRU approximated rather than exact, and why?
options: Exact LRU is free in hardware for every access
LRU is never approximated on any modern OS
True LRU per access is expensive; hardware/OS use reference bits or CLOCK
Only FIFO needs approximation; LRU is exact always
questions.explanation: Tracking every reference perfectly is costly; CLOCK/aging are common stand-ins.

questions.stem: Belady's anomaly: which algorithm can fault more with more frames, and what do you contrast?
options: LRU always faults more when frames increase
Only SJF scheduling exhibits Belady's anomaly
Belady means deadlock when page tables grow
FIFO can show Belady; stack algorithms like LRU do not
questions.explanation: FIFO is not a stack algorithm; adding frames can raise faults in some traces.

questions.stem: TLB shootdown on multicore: why mention it when discussing address-space changes?
options: Shootdown only clears L1 data cache, never TLBs
Other cores may cache stale translations; they must invalidate
Single-core systems need shootdowns for every syscall
Shootdown replaces page tables with segmentation only
questions.explanation: Remote TLB invalidation is part of the cost of unmap/remap on SMP.

questions.stem: Kernel preemption vs non-preemptive kernel: what tradeoff do you articulate?
options: Preemptive kernels never need locks in kernel code
Non-preemptive kernels always have lower interrupt latency
Preemption improves responsiveness but needs careful kernel locking
They are identical for latency and concurrency design
questions.explanation: Preemptible kernels raise concurrency complexity to cut long kernel-path stalls.

questions.stem: Spinlock vs mutex: when is spinning the interview-correct choice on multiprocessors?
options: Short critical sections on multiprocessors where blocking costs more
Always; spinning never wastes CPU cycles
Only on uniprocessors for long I/O waits
Spinlocks sleep the thread until the lock is free
questions.explanation: Spin if hold time is tiny vs context-switch cost; otherwise block/sleep.

questions.stem: Reader-writer lock: what pitfall do you flag for write-heavy or fairness-sensitive code?
options: Readers always serialize behind a single global mutex forever
Writers may starve if readers keep arriving without fairness policy
RW locks forbid concurrent readers by design
They eliminate all cache-line contention automatically
questions.explanation: Unbounded shared locks can starve writers; need writer preference or aging.

questions.stem: Memory-mapped files vs read/write syscalls: what concurrency/consistency point matters?
options: mmap never shares pages with the page cache
read/write cannot update file contents on disk
Writes via mmap may need explicit msync for durability visibility
mmap removes the need for any page faults ever
questions.explanation: Mapped dirty pages are not always flushed; durability needs sync discipline.

questions.stem: Soft IRQ / bottom half idea: why split interrupt handling this way on busy devices?
options: Move all device work into the hard IRQ with no deferral
Eliminate the need for any driver locking
Replace the scheduler with interrupt-only execution
Keep hard IRQ short; defer heavier work so interrupts stay responsive
questions.explanation: Top half acknowledges hardware quickly; bottom half does the bulk later.

questions.stem: OOM killer vs overcommit: how do you frame the Linux-ish tradeoff in an interview?
options: Overcommit means every malloc is physically pinned immediately
Overcommit can delay failure until touch time; OOM may kill to reclaim
OOM killer only runs when swap is completely disabled forever
Virtual memory forbids any form of overcommit by definition
questions.explanation: Optimistic allocation can fail later under pressure; policy decides who dies.

questions.stem: Two-level page tables: what problem do they solve versus a giant flat table?
options: Sparse address spaces avoid allocating unused upper-level entries
They remove the need for a TLB completely
They waste more memory than a dense flat table always
They make page faults impossible by design
questions.explanation: Hierarchical tables allocate only for used regions of a sparse VA space.

questions.stem: Futex-style user-space locking: what is the key performance idea behind the design?
options: Always trap to the kernel on every lock acquire and release
Locks live only in kernel and never touch atomics
Fast path in user space; kernel wait only on contention
User space cannot use atomic compare-and-swap
questions.explanation: Uncontended locks avoid syscalls; contended threads sleep in the kernel.

questions.stem: I/O-bound vs CPU-bound scheduling: what adjustment do you propose in words?
options: Always run CPU-bound before any I/O-bound job
Ignore burst behavior; only arrival time matters
Disable preemption for all interactive workloads
Favor I/O-bound for interactivity; give CPU-bound longer when fair
questions.explanation: I/O waiters need quick CPU slices to issue next I/O and stay responsive.

questions.stem: Deadlock prevention vs avoidance: how do you cleanly separate the two strategies?
options: Avoidance deletes locks; prevention only uses Banker's algorithm
Prevention breaks a Coffman condition; avoidance checks safe states (e.g., Banker)
They are synonyms with identical mechanisms
Prevention requires knowing future requests; avoidance never does
questions.explanation: Prevention redesigns resource rules; avoidance admits only safe allocations.

questions.stem: Huge pages: what benefit and cost do you mention when tuning a large heap?
options: Huge pages always increase TLB misses dramatically
They disable virtual memory for the process entirely
Fewer TLB misses from larger mappings; cost is fragmentation and setup complexity
Only useful for files under one kilobyte in size
questions.explanation: Larger pages cover more VA per TLB entry but are harder to allocate densely.

questions.stem: Senior pitfall: after a fork+exec failure path, why is leaking file descriptors across the child a production landmine?
options: Inherited FDs can keep sockets/files open, blocking restarts and exhausting limits
Children never inherit descriptors; leaks are impossible
Only stdin can leak; sockets are never inherited
FD inheritance is disabled unless you call mmap
questions.explanation: Close-on-exec and careful FD hygiene prevent accidental inheritance bugs.

questions.stem: Tradeoff talk: why might a senior reject naive global locking around a hot shared structure?
options: Global locks always scale linearly with cores
They serialize throughput and create cache-line ping-pong under contention
Lock-free is always simpler than a mutex for every team
Kernel schedulers ignore blocked threads on contended locks
questions.explanation: Coarse locks limit parallelism; sharding or finer locks may be needed.

questions.stem: You see rising major faults under load: what senior diagnosis steps do you outline?
options: Assume CPU scheduler bug only; ignore memory metrics
Disable the TLB permanently to reduce faults
Check RSS vs working set, swap activity, and whether thrashing explains latency
Major faults always mean deadlock, never memory pressure
questions.explanation: Correlate paging/swap with latency before blaming application logic alone.

questions.stem: ABA and lock-free stacks: what do you warn the interviewer you must handle?
options: ABA cannot happen with compare-and-swap on any architecture
ABA only affects mutexes, never lock-free structures
Hazard pointers are unused in real systems
A recycled pointer can make CAS succeed incorrectly without tags/epochs
questions.explanation: Pointer reuse fools CAS; versions, hazard pointers, or epochs mitigate ABA.

questions.stem: NUMA-aware allocation: what tradeoff do you state for a memory-heavy service?
options: NUMA is irrelevant once you have a TLB
Remote memory access can add latency; bind threads/memory to local nodes when possible
Always allocate only on the farthest node for fairness
Page migration never helps locality under NUMA
questions.explanation: Cross-node hops cost; locality-aware placement can cut tail latency.

questions.stem: False sharing: how do you explain the performance bug to a senior interviewer?
options: Independent variables on one cache line bounce between cores under writes
Sharing a mutex always eliminates cache traffic
False sharing only occurs with read-only data
It means two processes mapped the same file incorrectly
questions.explanation: Pad/align hot fields so writers do not invalidate each other's lines.

questions.stem: Async signal safety: why is malloc/printf inside a signal handler a senior red flag?
options: They are always async-signal-safe on every libc
Signals never interrupt threads holding locks
They may reenter non-reentrant state and deadlock or corrupt the heap
Only network syscalls are unsafe; allocators are fine
questions.explanation: Handlers must stick to async-signal-safe APIs or set flags for later work.

questions.stem: Thundering herd on a contended futex/accept: what mitigation narrative do you give?
options: Always wake all waiters; herds improve throughput
Disable interrupts to avoid wakeups entirely
Herds only happen with spinlocks, never blocking waits
Wake one waiter or shard the wait queue instead of waking everyone
questions.explanation: Broadcast wakes waste CPU; targeted wake or load distribution scales better.

questions.stem: Container memory limits vs host overcommit: what surprising failure mode do you call out?
options: cgroup limits never interact with the OOM killer
A container can be OOM-killed while the host still looks fine overall
Limits only affect disk IOPS, never RSS
Virtual memory ignores cgroup constraints completely
questions.explanation: Per-cgroup pressure can kill the workload despite spare host RAM.

questions.stem: Design tradeoff: blocking I/O thread pool vs evented loop—what do you say is the real decision?
options: Event loops always win for CPU-bound number crunching
They are identical under the kernel scheduler always
Thread pools simplify blocking calls; evented scales connections with careful nonblocking design
Blocking I/O cannot use a thread pool by definition
questions.explanation: Match model to workload: many idle connections favor events; blocking libs favor pools.
